panels.title: CPU usage
panels.description: JVM process CPU usage (0–1) and total host CPU across all cores. Normal: process CPU well below 1.0 under steady load. Problem: process CPU near 1.0 = CPU-bound (check Workflow Task Execution Latency); system CPU high but process low = another process stealing cycles; CPU low but latency high = I/O or thread contention (check JVM Thread States).

panels.title: Memory usage
panels.description: JVM heap used vs max, and non-heap (Metaspace, CodeCache). Normal: heap fluctuates with GC but stays well below max. Problem: heap approaching max = GC pressure causing latency spikes; monotonically growing heap = memory leak (check Sticky Cache Size and Kafka buffers). Note: JVM thread stacks (~1 MB each) live off-heap and are not shown here.

panels.title: RPC Requests Vs Failures
panels.description: Total rate of short gRPC calls from the SDK to the Temporal server, and how many fail, per namespace. Normal: failure rate near 0. Problem: any failures → check RPC Failures Per Operation; failures on StartWorkflowExecution = server rejecting new workflows (RPS limit) → check Service Errors by Type.

panels.title: RPC Failures Per Operation
panels.description: Failure rate of individual gRPC operations. Normal: all series at 0. Problem: PollWorkflowTaskQueue/PollActivityTaskQueue failures = worker cannot reach server; GetWorkflowExecutionHistory failures = replay failing. Correlate with Service Errors by Type for the server-side view.

panels.title: RPC Requests Per Operation
panels.description: Request rate per individual gRPC operation. Normal: Poll operations dominate; StartWorkflowExecution tracks your traffic rate. Problem: high GetWorkflowExecutionHistory rate = frequent replays from scratch → check Sticky Cache Miss and Sticky Cache Forced Eviction.

panels.title: RPC Latencies (p95)
panels.description: p95 round-trip latency of short gRPC calls from the SDK to the Temporal server, in ms. Normal: below 50ms in a local setup. Problem: rising on StartWorkflowExecution or SignalWorkflowExecution = Temporal frontend is slow → check Service Latency p95 and Persistence Latency p95.

panels.title: Long RPC Failures Per Operation
panels.description: Failure rate of long-poll gRPC calls (PollWorkflowTaskQueue, PollActivityTaskQueue) — the operations that deliver tasks to workers. Normal: 0. Problem: any failures = workers cannot sustain poll connection (network or server rejection).

panels.title: Long RPC Latencies (p95)
panels.description: p95 latency of long-poll operations (PollWorkflowTaskQueue, PollActivityTaskQueue), in ms. Normal: near 20,000ms when idle (that is the 20s poll timeout, not a problem). Problem: consistently much lower than 20s AND empty polls near 0 = workers always getting tasks (high load) → check Slots Available and Task Queue Backlog.

panels.title: Workflow Completion
panels.description: Rate of workflows reaching a successful terminal state, per workflow type. Normal: tracks Workflows Started/sec with a lag equal to average workflow duration. Problem: start rate >> completion rate sustained = backlog growing → check Workflows In Progress, Task Queue Backlog, Slots Available.

p95 time from workflow start to terminal state, in ms. Workflows that wait for external signals have high end-to-end latency by design. Problem: growing over time under same load = degradation → check JVM Thread States and Activity Execution Latency; sudden jump → check Activity Failed and RPC Latencies.
panels.title: Workflow End-To-End Latencies
Workflow Failures By Type
panels.description: Rate of workflows reaching a failed terminal state. Normal: 0 or low. Problem: sustained failures = unhandled exceptions or exhausted retries (check app logs); spike after deployment = non-determinism introduced by new code (use Workflow.getVersion() for history-safe migration).

panels.title: Workflows Started / sec
panels.description: Rate of new workflow executions being started — your inbound traffic rate. Problem: rate is 0 when traffic is expected → check RPC Requests vs Failures; rate far exceeds Workflow Completion/sec → backlog accumulating → check Workflows In Progress.

panels.title: Workflows Terminal States / sec
panels.description: All terminal outcomes in one panel: completed, failed, cancelled, continue-as-new. The total drain rate of the system. Problem: all terminal rates near 0 while started rate is non-zero = total blockage → check Slots Available, Task Queue Backlog, JVM Thread States.

panels.title: Workflows In Progress
panels.description: Estimated count of workflow executions in a non-terminal state (derived from cumulative counters; resets on worker restart). Java SDK note: each in-progress workflow in the sticky cache holds at least one real JVM thread in WAITING state — a large count here directly explains elevated JVM Thread States (waiting). Problem: never drops after traffic stops = workflows stuck (signal never arriving, stuck activity).

panels.title: Payment Rejections / sec by Reason
panels.description: [Custom — replace with your rejection counter] Rate of business-level rejections broken down by reason label. Normal: baseline rejection rate is business-defined. Problem: one reason spiking = systematic failure on that path; all reasons spiking together = systemic failure → check Workflow Failures By Type and Activity Failed.

panels.title: Task Queue Backlog — payment_queue
panels.description: [Custom — replace task queue name] Approximate count of tasks queued on the server not yet picked up by a worker.

panels.title: Workflow Task Throughput
panels.description: Rate of workflow tasks successfully polled and executed. Each workflow event (signal received, activity completed, timer fired) generates one task. Problem: drops to 0 while workflows are active = workers stopped polling → check Workflow Task Empty Polls.

panels.title: Workflow Task Schedule To Start (p95)
panels.description: p95 time between server queuing a workflow task and a worker picking it up, in ms. Normal: below 100ms (sticky execution keeps this near-instant). Problem: rising + Slots Available still high = server bottleneck → check Service Latency p95; rising + Slots Available at 0 = worker saturated → check Workflows In Progress and JVM Thread States (Java SDK: each in-progress workflow holds a thread slot).

Rate of workflow tasks that failed during execution. Normal: 0 — any non-zero value is serious. Problem: failures = non-determinism in workflow code (check app logs for NonDeterministicException); spike after deployment = new code incompatible with existing histories → use Workflow.getVersion().
panels.title: Workflow Task Failed
Workflow Task Execution Latency (p95)
panels.description: p95 time to run workflow code from a task start to the next blocking point, in ms. Normal: below 50ms — workflow code should only advance state. Problem: high = workflow code doing heavy computation or blocking I/O (never use Thread.sleep() in workflow code); correlated with CPU spikes = GC pauses.

panels.title: Workflow Task Replay Latency (p95)
panels.description: p95 time to replay a workflow's full history from scratch when not in the sticky cache, in ms. Problem: growing over time = history length growing (use continue_as_new to truncate near the 50,000 event limit); high replay AND Sticky Cache Miss rising = workers replaying too often → increase workflowCacheSize.

panels.title: Workflow Task Empty Polls
panels.description: Rate of long-poll requests for workflow tasks that returned empty (20s window expired, no task). Normal: high empty poll rate is healthy when idle — workers are ready and waiting. Problem: empty polls drop to 0 AND Task Queue Backlog is non-zero = tasks queued but no worker picking them up → check Slots Available.

panels.title: Activity Throughput
panels.description: Rate of activities successfully dispatched to and started by workers. Problem: drops to 0 while workflows are active = no activities dispatched → check Slots Available (ActivityWorker) and Empty Activity Polls.

panels.title: Activity Failed
panels.description: Rate of activity executions that threw an exception, per activity type. Temporal retries per the configured retry policy. Normal: 0 or low. Problem: sustained failures on one type = that dependency is down; all types failing = systemic issue; exhausted retries will cause Workflow Failures By Type to spike.

panels.title: Activity Execution Latencies (p95)
panels.description: p95 time from activity start to return, per activity type, in ms. Problem: growing on one type = that dependency is slowing down; high latency AND JVM Thread States (waiting) growing = activities are blocking JVM threads waiting for I/O — each concurrent activity holds one thread for its full duration.

panels.title: Empty Activity Polls
panels.description: Rate of long-poll requests for activity tasks that returned empty. Normal: high empty poll rate is healthy when idle — activity workers are ready and waiting. Problem: empty polls drop to 0 AND Task Queue Backlog (Activity) growing = activity workers not picking up tasks → check Slots Available (ActivityWorker).

targets.expr: histogram_quantile(0.95, sum by (namespace, task_queue, activity_type, le) (rate(temporal_activity_schedule_to_start_latency_seconds_bucket{namespace=~"$namespace"}[$__rate_interval]))) * 1000

panels.title: Activity Schedule To Start (p95)
panels.description: p95 time from Temporal scheduling an activity to a worker picking it up, in ms. Pure queueing time — the activity has not started yet. Problem: rising + Slots Available (ActivityWorker) at 0 = activity workers saturated → check Activity Execution Latency (slow activities hold slots longer); rising + Slots Available still high = server-side dispatch delay → check Service Latency p95.

panels.title: Slots Available
panels.description: Number of free task execution slots per worker type (WorkflowWorker and ActivityWorker). Normal: well above 0. Problem: approaching 0 = worker saturated. Java SDK caveat: slots NOT at 0 does NOT mean the worker is healthy — each in-progress workflow holds a real JVM thread in WAITING state regardless of slot count. Also check JVM Thread States and Sticky Cache Size; the real limit is maxWorkflowThreadCount (default 600).

panels.title: Slots Used
panels.description: Number of task execution slots currently occupied, per worker type. Complement of Slots Available. Problem: at maximum (= Slots Available at 0) = saturated; steadily growing = tasks running slower than arriving → check Activity Execution Latency.

panels.title: Sticky Cache Size
panels.description: Number of workflow executions held in the worker's in-memory sticky cache. Java SDK: each cached workflow holds at least one real JVM thread in WAITING state. Normal: grows with active workflows, bounded by workflowCacheSize (default 10,000). Problem: at the limit = evictions begin → check Sticky Cache Forced Eviction; high sticky cache AND high Memory Usage = workflow state consuming significant heap.

panels.title: Sticky Cache Forced Eviction
panels.description: Rate of workflows forcibly removed from the sticky cache to free space. When evicted, the next task must replay from full history. Normal: 0 or very low. Problem: sustained rate = workflowCacheSize too small → increase it; high eviction AND high Workflow Task Replay Latency = replays are expensive and causing latency.

panels.title: Sticky Cache Hit
panels.description: Rate of workflow tasks served directly from the sticky cache (no replay needed). Normal: high and stable. Problem: dropping = cache is being evicted too often → check Sticky Cache Forced Eviction; near 0 = caching is effectively disabled (workflowCacheSize too small or worker restarts frequently).

panels.title: Sticky Cache Miss
panels.description: Rate of workflow tasks not found in the sticky cache, requiring full history replay. Normal: low. Problem: high AND Sticky Cache Forced Eviction rising = cache too small; high WITHOUT evictions = workflows being routed to workers that do not have them cached (sticky queue routing issue).

panels.title: Task Dispatch Timeouts (poll_timeouts)
panels.description: Rate of tasks on the Temporal server that expired before any worker picked them up. Replace `payment_queue` with your task queue name. Normal: 0. Problem: non-zero + empty polls > 0 = workers alive but cannot keep up; non-zero + empty polls = 0 = workers stopped polling → check Slots Available.

panels.title: Service Request Rate
panels.description: Rate of internal gRPC calls handled by each Temporal server component. Frontend handles client/worker API calls; History manages workflow state transitions; Matching dispatches tasks to workers. Problem: History rate very high = heavy replays → check Sticky Cache Miss; any component drops to 0 = that component has stopped.

panels.title: Service Errors by Type
panels.description: Rate of server-side errors broken down by error type and component. Normal: serviceerror_NotFound on SignalWorkflowExecution is expected (signal to already-completed workflow). Problem: ResourceExhausted = server RPS limits hit (seen in server logs as 'Per shard RPS warn limit exceeded'); serviceerror_Unavailable = server or DB unreachable.

panels.title: Service Latency p95 (ms)
panels.description: p95 latency of internal gRPC operations within the Temporal server per component, in ms. Normal: frontend < 20ms, history < 50ms, matching < 50ms. Problem: history latency rising = DB is the bottleneck → check Persistence Latency p95; frontend latency rising = client-facing calls slow → will appear as elevated RPC Latencies.

panels.title: Persistence Latency p95 (ms)
panels.description: p95 latency of database (Postgres) operations per operation type, in ms. Temporal writes to DB on every workflow state transition. Normal: below 5ms in a local setup. Problem: any operation above 10ms sustained = Temporal server slows globally; UpdateWorkflowExecution is the most frequent write — its latency is the primary DB bottleneck signal.

panels.title: Persistence Errors
panels.description: Rate of database errors per operation and error category. Normal: serviceerror_NotFound on GetTaskQueue at startup is expected. Problem: any write operation errors = workflows may fail or get stuck silently → investigate DB health immediately.

panels.title: Worker Saturation — Schedule-to-Start / Slots / Backlog
panels.description: All worker saturation signals in one panel. Left axis: schedule-to-start p95 (ms) for workflow and activity tasks. Right axis: slots available and backlog. Pattern 1 (worker saturated): schedule-to-start rising + slots at 0 + backlog growing. Pattern 2 (server bottleneck): schedule-to-start rising + slots still high + empty polls non-zero → check Service Latency p95. Pattern 3 (no pollers): backlog growing + empty polls = 0 + pollers = 0 → workers crashed.

panels.title: Kafka Producer: Message Rate
panels.description: Standard Kafka producer metrics. Rate of records the producer attempts to send per second, and send errors. Filter by client_id to match your producer. Normal: error rate is 0. Problem: error rate > 0 = messages failing to reach broker → check Kafka Producer: Buffer Pressure; send rate drops while Activity Throughput stays normal = Kafka-producing activity failing before send → check Activity Failed.

panels.title: Kafka Producer: Throughput
panels.description: Standard Kafka producer metric. Bytes per second leaving the Kafka producer. Filter by client_id. Normal: proportional to message rate × average message size. Problem: bytes drop while record rate stays same = messages getting smaller; both drop = throughput starvation → check Kafka Producer: Buffer Pressure.

panels.title: Kafka Producer: Latency
panels.description: Standard Kafka producer metric. Average round-trip latency of a produce request to the broker and back, in ms. Filter by client_id. Normal: below 5ms with acks=1 in a local setup. Problem: rising = broker is slow → compare with Broker Produce Latency in the Kafka Cluster dashboard; high AND Activity Execution Latency elevated = activity blocking a JVM thread waiting for ACK → check JVM Thread States.

panels.title: Kafka Consumer: Message Rate
panels.description: Standard Kafka consumer metric. Rate of records consumed per second per topic. Filter by topic. Normal: tracks the produce rate; consumer lag stays near 0. Problem: rate drops = consumer slow or stopped → check Kafka Consumer: Lag; rate is 0 = consumer not running or lost partition assignment → check Rebalance Latency.

panels.title: Kafka Consumer: Lag
panels.description: Standard Kafka consumer metric. Records produced but not yet consumed, per topic. Filter by topic. Normal: near 0. Problem: growing = consumer cannot keep up; lag is 0 AND message rate is 0 AND producer is producing = consumer is not running.

panels.title: Kafka Consumer: Throughput
panels.description: Standard Kafka consumer metric. Bytes per second incoming to the Kafka consumer. Filter by client_id. Normal: proportional to message rate × average message size.

panels.title: Kafka Consumer: Latency
panels.description: Standard Kafka consumer metric. Average time the consumer waits for the broker to return a fetch response, in ms. Filter by client_id. Normal: below 100ms. Problem: very high = broker is slow, or consumer configured with large fetch.max.wait.ms.

panels.title: Kafka Consumer: Rebalance Latency
panels.description: Standard Kafka consumer metric. Average duration of consumer group rebalancing events. Rebalances temporarily pause all consumption. Filter by client_id. Normal: rare and short (below 1s). Problem: frequent or long rebalances = consumers crashing and rejoining; rebalance spike coincides with Kafka Consumer: Lag spike = consumption was paused.

panels.title: Rejected Payments — Total by Reason
panels.description: [Custom — replace with your rejection counter] Cumulative total rejected payments grouped by reason since startup. Unlike the rate panel, this shows the running total for post-incident review. Problem: one reason disproportionately large = that path has a systematic problem.

panels.title: Kafka Producer: Waiting Threads
panels.description: Standard Kafka producer metric. Number of threads blocked waiting for buffer space in the producer's record accumulator — before the message is even sent. Filter by client_id. Normal: 0. Problem: > 0 = producer buffer full, broker cannot consume fast enough → check Kafka Producer: Buffer Pressure. Distinct from ACK blocking: this is buffer allocation (before send), not waiting for broker ACK.

panels.title: Kafka Producer: Buffer Pressure
panels.description: Standard Kafka producer metrics. bufferpool_wait_ratio: fraction of time the producer spends waiting for buffer space (0 = no pressure, 1 = always waiting). buffer_exhausted_total: times the buffer was completely full. Filter by client_id. Normal: wait_ratio near 0, exhausted static. Problem: wait_ratio > 0.1 sustained → check Kafka Producer: Latency; exhausted counter incrementing = buffer full, messages at risk of being dropped.

panels.title: JVM Thread States — Activity Worker Threads

panels.description: JVM thread count broken down by lifecycle state: waiting, timed-waiting, blocked, runnable. Counts all threads — Temporal SDK, activity threads, Vert.x, thread pools. Normal: waiting has a stable baseline (~20–30) from idle pool workers; grows proportionally with Workflows In Progress in the Temporal Java SDK (each cached workflow holds at least one real JVM thread in WAITING state — by design). Problem: waiting grows faster than Workflows In Progress = activities holding extra threads for I/O; blocked > 0 sustained = monitor contention; waiting approaching 600 (default maxWorkflowThreadCount) = worker near thread limit.
panels.title: JVM Thread States (stacked)

panels.description: Right axis: standard JVM metric — total threads in waiting + timed-waiting states (all parked threads regardless of reason). Left axis: [Custom — replace with your blocking operation timer] avg duration of a specific application-level blocking call in ms. In this setup it is the fraud Kafka send. Normal: right axis tracks Workflows In Progress (Temporal sticky thread baseline); left axis low when the dependency is fast. Problem: left axis rising AND right axis rising faster than Workflows In Progress = the blocking call is contributing extra thread accumulation; right axis approaching 600 = worker near maxWorkflowThreadCount limit.
panels.title: JVM Parked Threads (waiting + timed-waiting)
